title: Dynamic Programming: Introduction
description: Build the DP mindset from scratch — learn to identify overlapping subproblems, formulate recurrences, and transition fluently between top-down memoization and bottom-up tabulation to crack Codeforces problems up to rating 1600.

sections.content: 🧠 What Is Dynamic Programming?

**Dynamic Programming (DP)** is an algorithm design technique for problems with two key properties: **overlapping subproblems** (the same sub-calculation is needed multiple times) and **optimal substructure** (an optimal solution is composed of optimal solutions to subproblems). DP eliminates redundant recomputation by storing results — turning exponential brute-force into polynomial solutions. On Codeforces, DP is the single most important topic for reaching 1600+ rating.

sections.label: DP Recognition Pattern
sections.content: A problem likely has a DP solution if it asks: **minimum/maximum** cost of doing something, **number of ways** to achieve something, or **whether it is possible** to reach a state — and the constraints are small enough (n ≤ 10^3 to 10^5 depending on dimensions). Greedy fails? Think DP.
🔑 Two Implementations: Memoization vs Tabulation

Every DP can be written either **top-down** (memoization: recursion + cache) or **bottom-up** (tabulation: iterative, fill table in dependency order). Both are O(states × cost-per-transition). Top-down is often easier to derive; bottom-up is usually faster in practice (no recursion overhead, better cache locality).

rows: Either; tabulation preferred at high levels

sections.content: 🪜 Step-by-Step DP Framework

items: **Define the state**: What does `dp[i]` (or `dp[i][j]`) represent? Be precise.
**Write the recurrence**: How does `dp[i]` depend on smaller states?
**Identify base cases**: What are the trivially known values?
**Determine the answer**: Which state(s) hold the final answer?
**Choose top-down or bottom-up**: Verify fill order for tabulation.
**Analyze complexity**: States × transitions. Verify it fits within time limits.

sections.content: 🎯 Canonical Example 1: Fibonacci (Warm-Up)
Fibonacci is the "Hello World" of DP. Naive recursion is O(2^n); with memoization it becomes O(n). While trivial, it perfectly illustrates the two DP implementations.

sections.title: Fibonacci — Top-Down vs Bottom-Up

sections.content: 🪙 Canonical Example 2: Coin Change (Minimum Coins)

**Problem**: Given coin denominations and a target amount, find the minimum number of coins needed. This is the poster child for DP — greedy fails for non-canonical coin systems (e.g., coins `{1,3,4}`, target `6`: greedy gives `4+1+1=3` coins, DP gives `3+3=2` coins).

sections.title: Coin Change — Bottom-Up Tabulation

sections.label: #1 DP Bug in Contests
sections.content: Initializing the DP array to `0` instead of `INT_MAX`/`INF` for minimization problems (or to `INF` for maximization). Also: when using `INF = INT_MAX`, doing `dp[i-c] + 1` overflows to a negative number — always use a safe sentinel like `1e9` and check `dp[i-c] != INF` before adding.
🧮 Canonical Example 3: 0/1 Knapsack (2D DP)
The **0/1 Knapsack** problem introduces 2D DP states. Each item can either be taken (0) or not taken (1). State: `dp[i][w]` = maximum value using first `i` items with weight capacity `w`.

sections.title: 0/1 Knapsack — 2D Tabulation + Space Optimization

sections.label: 0/1 vs Unbounded Knapsack
sections.content: In **0/1 knapsack**, iterate weights **backwards** (prevents reuse of same item). In **unbounded knapsack** (unlimited copies of each item), iterate weights **forwards**. Getting this direction wrong is an extremely common contest mistake.
📈 Canonical Example 4: Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS)

**LIS** is a landmark DP problem. O(n²) DP is straightforward; the O(n log n) patience-sort approach using binary search is the contest standard. Understanding LIS deeply prepares you for dozens of related problems.

sections.title: LIS — O(n²) DP and O(n log n) Optimized

sections.content: 📊 DP Complexity Reference

rows: dp[i] = min coins for amount i
dp[i] = min(dp[i-c]+1) for c in coins
dp[i][w] = max val, i items, capacity w
dp[i] = LIS length ending at i

sections.content: 🗺️ Grid DP: Paths in a Grid

Grid DP is one of the most common introductory DP patterns. Count the number of paths from top-left to bottom-right moving only right or down. State: `dp[i][j]` = number of paths to reach cell `(i,j)`.

sections.title: Count Paths in Grid (with Obstacles)

sections.content: 🧪 Interactive Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Trace the Coin Change DP Table
Exercise 2: Trace the LIS O(n²) DP
Exercise 3: Maximum Sum Subarray (Kadane's Algorithm as DP)
sections.approach: DP state: `dp[i]` = maximum subarray sum ending exactly at index `i`. Recurrence: `dp[i] = max(a[i], dp[i-1] + a[i])` — either start a new subarray at i, or extend the previous best. Answer = max over all dp[i]. This is Kadane's algorithm. Space-optimize to O(1) by keeping only `cur` and `best`.
sections.explanation:
**State**: `cur` represents `dp[i]` — max subarray sum ending at `i`. Recurrence: extend previous (`cur + a[i]`) or start fresh (`a[i]`), take the maximum.
**Trace on example**: a={-2,1,-3,4,-1,2,1,-5,4}. cur/best: -2/-2, 1/1, -2/1, 4/4, 3/4, 5/5, 6/6, 1/6, 5/6. Answer = **6**.
**All-negative case**: Starting `best = a[0]` handles this correctly — we take the least-negative element.
**Complexity**: O(n) time, O(1) space — optimal.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Count Ways to Climb Stairs
sections.approach: DP state: `dp[i]` = number of ways to reach stair `i`. Base cases: `dp[0]=1` (one way to stand at start), `dp[1]=1`. Recurrence: `dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]` — we arrived from stair `i-1` (took 1 step) or from stair `i-2` (took 2 steps). This is Fibonacci(n+1).

sections.title: Exercise 5: Codeforces-Style — Minimum Cost Path in Grid
sections.approach: DP state: `dp[i][j]` = minimum cost to reach cell (i,j). Base: `dp[0][0] = grid[0][0]`. First row: `dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-1] + grid[0][j]` (only can come from left). First col: `dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0] + grid[i][0]` (only from above). General: `dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) + grid[i][j]`.
sections.explanation:
**Trace on example**: After filling:
```
dp:
1  4  5
2  7  6
6  8  7
```
dp[2][2] = **7** ✓ (path: 1→1→1→1→1→1→1 does not exist; 1→3→1→1→1=7 via top row then down right)
**Complexity**: O(n×m) time and space — optimal. Can space-optimize to O(m) using rolling array if n×m is large.
